coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots with long-wavelength and photoluminescence‐tunable emission to achieve multicolor imaging in cells
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Chaoge
Xing, Malcolm
Wu, Qilin
coredata.dc:description: Most carbon dots (CDs) derived from natural biowastes show emission limitedly in the blue range. This work applied urea and sulfuric acid as dopants to assist the carbonization of cellulose-based biowaste willow catkin. Then a facile chemical cutting method was developed for scalable production of CDs. Under excitation at a single-wavelength of 360 nm, the aqueous solutions of CDs obtained at different cutting temperatures (100, 120, and 140 °C) exhibited strong blue (486 nm), green (522 nm) and yellow (545 nm) emission, respectively. The as-prepared CDs also exhibited excitation-independent emission behaviors and high photostability in different pH and ionic strength. Furthermore, these long-wavelength and photoluminescence-tunable CDs were successfully applied to multicolor bioimaging in Hela cells, indicating their potential toward multimodal sensing applications in biological environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence‐tunable emission
Multi-color bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PL-tunable CDs from willow catkin
Cell cytotoxicity and intracellular multi-color imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the preparation of PL-tunable CDs from willow catkin.
TEM images of prepared (a) b-CDs, (b) g-CDs and (c) y-CDs. (d) XPS survey spectra and (e) FT-IR spectra of three CDs. (f) The Raman spectra of carbonized cellulose, b-CDs, g-CDs and y-CDs.
The high-resolution XPS spectra of b-CDs, g-CDs and y-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra of three CDs under 360 nm excitation (inset: the CDs aqueous solution under UV light). (b) The PL emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) The PL intensity variation of CDs under different pH values. (d) Effect of UV irradiation time on PL intensity of the resulting CDs.

(a) Cell viability assays of Hela cells after 24 h treatment with different concentrations of CDs. CLSM images of Hela cells incubated with (b) b-CDs, (c) g-CDs and (d) y-CDs, respectively. (C [b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs] = 40 μg/mL, λex = 405 nm).
Comparison of CDs from willow catkin and other natural resources.
Surface atomic percentage of three CDs from XPS.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots with long-wavelength and photoluminescence‐tunable emission to achieve multicolor imaging in cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, 201620, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Medical Genetics, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Manitoba, R3T 5V6, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Medical Genetics

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Room C378, College Building NO.5, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Road, Songjiang District, Shanghai, China.
Corresponding author. 504 John Buhler Research Centre, 715 McDermot Avenue, Winnipeg, Manitoba R3E 3P5, Canada.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most carbon dots (CDs) derived from natural biowastes show emission limitedly in the blue range. This work applied urea and sulfuric acid as dopants to assist the carbonization of cellulose-based biowaste willow catkin. Then a facile chemical cutting method was developed for scalable production of CDs. Under excitation at a single-wavelength of 360 nm, the aqueous solutions of CDs obtained at different cutting temperatures (100, 120, and 140 °C) exhibited strong blue (486 nm), green (522 nm) and yellow (545 nm) emission, respectively. The as-prepared CDs also exhibited excitation-independent emission behaviors and high photostability in different pH and ionic strength. Furthermore, these long-wavelength and photoluminescence-tunable CDs were successfully applied to multicolor bioimaging in Hela cells, indicating their potential toward multimodal sensing applications in biological environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of photoluminescence (PL) nanomaterials, have drawn considerable scientific attention due to their fascinating properties like small size, good water solubility, and high resistance to photobleaching [1–4]. Recently, CDs derived from green and inexpensive natural precursors, also known as biowaste based CDs, have attracted special attention owing to their additional advantages such as fascinating optical properties [5,6], superior biocompatibility, especially the low-cost production from abundant and cheap carbon resources compared to the conventional CDs and organic fluorescent dyes [7–9]. As such, the biowaste based CDs are of potential as a satisfactory candidate for biosensing and bioimaging applications [5,10].

Conspicuous contrast image is necessary for tracking at molecular level inside living system [10–12]. However, the photoluminescence (PL) color variation in CDs, especially the biowaste based CDs, is less pronounced compared with that found in traditional semiconducting quantum dots. CDs from biowastes mostly show emission in the blue range (Table .1 ), which can interfere with the fluorescent signals from cells and tissues [2,5], and give rise to fundamental limitations to their effective applications in biolabeling and bioimaging applications. In the past few years, a variety of carbohydrates and polymers have been used to obtain CDs with PL-tunable emitting properties [13,14]. However, there are few reports on PL-tunable CDs from biowaste. Herein, an attempt has been made to synthesize the PL-tunable CDs from biomass cellulose, which is the most abundant and cheap organic polymer on the Earth, and easy to access [15].

In order to obtain long-wavelength emitting CDs and enhance their competitiveness in bioimaging applications, herein, our work was trying to synthesize PL-tunable CDs from cellulose-based biowaste willow catkin, which is one of the representative and useless cellulose-based biowaste in northern China [16]. Under the combined synergistic effects of urea and sulfuric acid assisted carbonization, and chemical cutting treatment, the PL emission of resulting CDs was expected to be well-tuned, thus, multicolor bioimaging in Hela cells was attempted as well.

Cellulose-based biowaste willow catkin was collected from the campus of Donghua University (Shanghai, China). CCK-8 Kit, Dulbecco's modified Eagle (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum were purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). sulfuric acid of 98.0 wt.%, nitric acid of 65.0 wt.% and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PL-tunable CDs from willow catkin
ce:section.ce:para: The long-wavelength and PL-tunable emission CDs were prepared by chemical cutting treatment of carbonized willow catkin with different cutting temperatures (Fig. 1 ). Specifically, willow catkin was firstly soaked in mixed aqueous solution of urea (0.2 M) and sulfuric acid (0.6 M) at room temperature for 12 h, then dried at 60 °C for another 12 h. The treated willow catkin was pre-oxidized from room temperature to 250 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min in the carbonizing furnace under air and then further carbonized at 900 °C for 20 min under nitrogen atmosphere. The carbonized willow catkin (0.4 g) was added into 30 mL concentrated HNO3 and H2SO4 solution with a volume ratio of 1:3. Finally, the solution was heated with variable temperatures (100 °C, 120 °C or 140 °C) in an oil bath for 8 h under constant stirring to obtain CDs, and the CDs prepared at different temperatures were named as y-CDs (100 °C), g-CDs (120 °C) and b-CDs (140 °C), respectively.

After the chemical cutting treatment finished, the obtained CDs solution was followed by neutralization with Na2CO3 and then purified with filter membrane (0.22 μm) and dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da). It should be emphasized that the preparation approach is suitable for scalable production owing to the high production yield of the resulting CDs from willow catkin (Table .1). The quantum yield (QY) measurements, CCK assays and multi-color bioimaging details were given in supplementary material.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100F electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. Raman spectra were recorded on a confocal Raman system (Renishaw InVia Reflex) with a He-Ne laser at a power of 10 mW (633 nm). FT-IR spectra were recorded with a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transmission infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on an Escalab 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) using a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source (hv = 1458.6 eV). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a FP-6600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (JASCO, Japan). UV/Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Lambda 35 UV-vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). 1H NMR (nuclear magnetic resonance) spectra of CDs in D2O were acquired using a Bruker AVANCEIII HD-600 (Bruker, USA) instrument at ambient temperature.

In order to achieve multi-color emitting CDs, the tunable size distribution, appropriate heteroatom doping and oxidation degrees are necessary [2,22]. By introducing urea and sulfuric acid as dopants and adjusting the chemical cutting conditions, the obtained CDs, as shown in Fig. 2 a–c, were found to be nearly spherical in morphology, and the average diameters of b-CDs, g-CDs and y-CDs were around 2.40, 2.81 and 2.86 nm, respectively.

In addition, the XPS spectra were then employed to evaluate the surface states and composition of the as-prepared CDs. The XPS full scan spectra of the obtained CDs (Fig. 2d) all exhibited four typical peaks at 285 eV, 532 eV, 400 eV and 167 eV, which were attributed to C1 s, O1 s, N1 s and S2p, respectively [23,24]. The full scan spectra indicated that all the obtained CDs samples were consiste of the same elements and the contents of these elements were summarized in Table 2 . A comparative study showed that the CDs displayed a slightly higher inclusion of oxygen and lower inclusion of carbon when increased the chemical cutting temperature. These results suggested that from y-CDs to b-CDs, the degree of oxidation was increased, which might result in the differences of emission colors. Then, the high-resolution XPS spectra further revealed the detailed elements bonding nature of the three CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 , the C1S, N1s and S2P profiles of each CDs sample were almost the same regardless of the chemical bond species or the relative chemical bond content. It is specially noted that the O1s profile of the three CDs showed signals at 531 (C=O), and 532.1 eV (C-O), and the signal corresponding to C-O bond increased as the chemical cutting temperature decreased from 140 °C (b-CDs) to 100 °C (y-CDs). Different degrees of oxidation on the surfaces of three CDs samples resulted from different chemical cutting temperature, ultimately, to some extent, determining the colors of their PL [25].

Further composition features of three CDs were examined by FT-IR and 1H NMR spectrum analysis. As depicted in Fig. 2e, the b-CDs, g-CDs and y-CDs shared some similar absorption peaks, e.g. 3400 cm−1 (O-H/N-H stretching vibrations), 2923 cm−1 (C-H stretching vibrations) [26], and the peaks at 1720 cm−1, 1575 cm−1, 1420 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1, which are corresponding to the stretching vibrations of C=O, C=C, CH2 and the C-O groups, respectively [8,24]. The clearly reduced peak intensities of C-O bands from y-CDs to b-CDs (Fig. 2e) revealed that most of the epoxy functional groups were gradually destroyed with the elevated chemical cutting temperatures, which is well consistent with the O1s profiles in the high-resolution XPS analysis (Fig. 3). The structure composition of all three CDs was also examined by 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S1) and there were minor differences among them. The signals at 3.4–3.7 ppm and 4.7 ppm in the 1H NMR spectra further confirm the presence of protons attached with hydroxyl and carbonyl groups at the surface of CDs [27].

Moreover, typical Raman spectra exhibited two major peaks centered at 1345 cm−1 (D band) and 1581 cm−1 (G band) (Fig. 2f). The intensity ratios ID/IG, which is generally considered as a crucial factor in determining the graphitization degree of carbons, for carbonized cellulose, y-CDs, g-CDs and b-CDs were 1.36, 1.29, 1.20 and 1.18, respectively [15]. The decreased ID/IG values for Raman spectra of samples suggested that the increased chemical cutting temperature has resulted in more ordered graphitic structures [15].

The origin of the tunable emission still remains a debatable subject. Recent studies have shown that the different emission color of CDs may arise from the differences of surface states and sizes of CDs [28]. In this work, there existed differences among the obtained CDs in sizes, functional groups, and graphitic structures, which may change the optical bandgaps of CDs, thus enabling the adjustment of PL colors [25,29].

We were surprised to find that, under excitation at a single-wavelength of 360 nm, the aqueous solutions of b-CDs exhibited strong blue emission centered at 486 nm, g-CDs exhibited strong green emission centered at 522 nm, while y-CDs exhibited strong yellow emission centered at 545 nm (inset in Fig. 4 a). Such long-wavelength and multi-color emission CDs have rarely been synthesized from biowastes before (Table 1). The relative fluorescence QY of the obtained CDs were 4.5% for b-CDs, 6.9% for g-CDs and 5.6% for y-CDs (Fig. S2). In addition, the UV-Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4a) of three CDs all displayed broad absorption in the 200–500 nm range, and typical absorption peaks at 242 nm (π-π* transition of graphitic sp2 domains), which suggests that the sp2-hybridized honeycomb-like (C=C) CDs structure was obtained through carbonization and chemical cutting procedure [30].

More interestingly, different from the excitation-dependent PL behavior of most reported CDs (Table 1) [22,31], the PL spectra of our three CDs all showed excitation-independent emission character (Fig. 4b), that was, the emission peaks of b-CDs, g-CDs and y-CDs remained unshifted with the excitation wavelengths changing from 360 to 460 nm. This excitation-independent PL superiority might make the synthesized CDs appropriate candidates for bioimaging and biolabeling uses [32].

In order to extend the application potential of CDs as fluorescent labels, the PL stability of resulting CDs was also investigated. As revealed in Fig. 4c and Fig. S3, the PL behavior of CDs was proved to be rather stable in a wide range of pH values (pH 4–10). In addition, three CDs possessed outstanding PL stability even continuously irradiated by a 150 W UV-lamp (Fig. 4d) or in NaCl solutions (Fig. S4). Moreover, the electronic charge on the surface of the b-CDs, g-CDs and y-CDs (zeta potential) is −46.5 mV, −38.5 mV, and −37.6 mV, respectively (Fig. S5), which confirms that the obtained CDs possess outstanding dispersion stability, and will be beneficial to their stable sensing and bioimaging applications [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity and intracellular multi-color imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed CDs were synthesized from cellulose-based biowaste and incorporated without toxic elements may provide their biocompatibility for diverse applications in bioimaging and beyond [5]. To benefit from their excellent photostability and multi-color fluorescent emitting properties, the proposed CDs were tried to bioimage Hela cells [5]. The CCK assay results (Fig. 5 a) demonstrated that the CDs impose no obvious toxicity to Hela cells even at high dose (400 μg/mL), indicating that the CDs have low cytotoxicity and remarkable biocompatibility and safe for in vitro applications [33]. Fortunately, after Hela cells incubated with b-CDs, the cell cytoplasm region successfully displayed bright blue pattern (Fig. 5b), while green and yellow luminescence can also be clearly shown out once g-CDs and y-CDs were applied, respectively (Fig. 5c and d). These observations demonstrate that the obtained CDs were appropriate for tracking and biological imaging applications [22].

In summary, a facile and low-cost strategy is achieved in the controllable synthesis of blue, green and yellow emission fluorescent CDs from cellulose-based biowaste. This strategy shows prominent advantages of long-wavelength emitting and mass-manufacturability over the reported biomass-based CDs. The as-synthesized CDs showed excellent PL stability, low cytotoxicity, and more importantly, excitation-independent emission behaviors. Accordingly, the PL‐tunable CDs could be used for multimodal sensing in biological environment.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for National Key R&D Program of China (2016YFB0303201) & the Research and Innovation Project of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 14zz069). M.X. would like to thank the Discovery Grant of Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada and NSERC Discovery Accelerator Supplement Award.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2018.12.007.

Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo

Solvothermal conversion of coal into nitrogen-doped carbon dots with singlet oxygen generation and high quantum yield
Highly fluorescent carbon dots from enokitake mushroom as multi-faceted optical nanomaterials for Cr6+ and VOC detection and imaging applications
Facile and green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission for sensors and cells imaging
Bandgap engineering of nanosized carbon dots through electron-accepting functionalization
Nitrogen, sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dot: an excellent sensor for nitroexplosives
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism

A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Anomalous behaviors of visible luminescence from graphene quantum dots: interplay between size and shape
